<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The progress bar now appears at the top during scrolling across all pages.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

Done. Every page now has a fixed 3px reading progress bar at the very top of the viewport.

**What was added**

1. **`layouts/_partials/components/scroll-progress.html`** — thin bar markup, included from `baseof.html` on all pages
2. **`assets/js/scroll-progress.js`** — updates bar width from scroll position (same rAF-throttled pattern as back-to-top)
3. **`assets/css/custom.css`** — fixed positioning at `z-index: 60`, accent fill, subtle track, respects `prefers-reduced-motion`
4. **`hugo.toml`** — loads the script in the main bundle (`lazy = false`) so it runs immediately

The bar sits above the sticky header and fills left-to-right as you scroll. On short pages with nothing to scroll, it stays at 0%. It’s hidden in randomizer focus mode along with the other chrome.

Run `hugo server` and scroll a long blog post to see it in action.

---

_**User**_

use the same blue color at the progress bar at the back to top

---

Reading the back-to-top partial and custom.css to find the exact sky-500 color definition.</details></think>

The back-to-top progress ring uses `text-sky-500`, which applies `currentColor` to the stroke.

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The stroke width is already 9, yet the back-to-top element still appears too thin.

With a 48×48 SVG and a 40-pixel circle radius, a stroke width of 9 already places the outer edge almost flush against the SVG boundary. Further increases risk clipping.

A larger SVG or smaller inner button would create breathing room for a thicker ring. Shrinking the radius is another option. Stroke width 9 already clips because the stroke is centered on the path; the maximum safe value is roughly 8.

A 56×56 SVG with radius 24 and stroke width 6 would give the ring a much thicker, more solid appearance without clipping.

The JavaScript hardcodes a radius of 20, so any change to the SVG radius will require updating both the script and the HTML stroke-dasharray. A 56×56 SVG with center at 28, radius 22, and stroke width 8 keeps the outer edge safely inside the bounds.

The current 44-pixel button is larger than the inner ring hole, so the stroke wraps around it. A 56-pixel SVG with radius 22 would leave a 36-pixel inner gap, making the ring feel thinner rather than thicker. The inner button should stay small to preserve the ring’s visual thickness.

The 44-pixel button completely obscures the ring, leaving only the outer arc visible. A 56×56 SVG with radius 24 and stroke width 6 would place the ring entirely outside the button, yielding a 42-pixel inner diameter and a much thicker, more solid appearance.

A 60×60 SVG with radius 25 and stroke width 6 places the inner ring edge exactly at the button’s 22-pixel radius. This makes the ring fully visible around the button. The JavaScript radius constant must also be updated to 25.

A 60×60 SVG with radius 26 and stroke width 8 would place the inner ring edge exactly at the button’s 22-pixel radius, while the outer edge fits cleanly inside the bounds. The JavaScript radius constant must be updated to 26. The user may simply want the stroke width increased, since the current 9-pixel width is already clipped and hidden behind the button.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

The ring likely looks thin because a 44px button covers most of it inside the 48px SVG — I'll enlarge the ring geometry so more stroke shows.
